grammar.usage: Used with negative adverbials at the beginning of a sentence for emphasis. Verb order follows question word order.
grammar.example: Never have I worked so hard in all my life. / Hardly had Matt started work when he resigned.

grammar.usage: Used to emphasize descriptions of quality or intensity followed by a result.
grammar.example: So tough was the sea that the children could not go swimming. / Such a good book is it that I have read twice.

grammar.usage: Use 's for singular nouns/plural not ending in s, s' for plural ending in s, and 'of' for things/places.
grammar.example: That's the manager's car, over there. / The workers' pay was increased by 10%.

grammar.usage: Possessive adjectives are used before nouns. Possessive pronouns replace a noun or follow 'of'.
grammar.example: I'm really excited about my new job. / My new job is great. How about yours?

bullets: Inversion is used when negative adverbial words or phrases (cụm trạng từ phủ định) are placed at the beginning of a sentence for emphasis.
The word order of the subject and the verb changes to question word order (auxiliary verb before subject).
Inversion is only used in formal contexts and is rarely used in spoken language.

blocks.tableName: Inversion in the First Clause Reference
rows: Never / Rarely / Seldom...

Never / Rarely / Seldom + auxiliary verb + S + V...
EX: I have never worked so hard in all my life.
→ Never have I worked so hard in all my life.
→ Rarely have I worked so hard in all my life.
→ Seldom have I worked so hard in all my life.
No sooner (... than)

No sooner + auxiliary verb (often had) + S + V + than + S + V
EX: No sooner had I eaten shrimp than I started feeling sick.
EX: No sooner will Linda arrive than she will want to leave.
Hardly / Scarcely / Barely (... when)

Hardly / Scarcely / Barely + auxiliary verb + S + V + when + S + V
EX: Hardly had Matt started work when he resigned.
EX: Scarcely had Bill left the library when the phone rang.
Not only (... but also / too)

Not only + auxiliary verb + S + V + but (also) + S + V
EX: Not only can increasing cost of fuel save fuel, it also / too protects environment.
EX: Not only are video games costly, they are also harmful to your health.
EX: Not only does smoking harm your own health, it also threatens others.
In / Under no circumstances

In / Under no circumstances + auxiliary verb + S + V...
EX: In/Under no circumstances will I betray my own country.
EX: Under no circumstances are employees allowed to leave the building without permission.
At no time / point

At no time / point + auxiliary verb + S + V...
EX: At no time / point was I told what job involved.
Little + realise / know / suspect...

Little + auxiliary verb + S + realize / know / suspect...
EX: Little did I realize that I would become managing director just two years later.

blocks.tableName: Inversion in the Second Clause Reference
rows: Not until...

Not until + time / clause + auxiliary verb + S + V...
EX: Not until the next day did I hear that I had got the job.
Only...

Only + prepositional phrase / clause + auxiliary verb + S + V...
EX: Only at the end of interview did I think I had a chance of getting the job.

bullets: Used in short answers to agree with a previous statement.
**So** is used to agree with an affirmative statement.
**Neither / nor** is used to agree with a negative statement.

rows: So
EX: "I'm a plumber." "Really, so am I."
Neither / Nor
EX: My sister doesn't like getting ready for work, and neither do I / nor do I.

blocks.tableName: As, So, Such, and Conditionals Reference
rows: As, so, such...

EX1: The manager was nervous about the director's visit, as were the rest of the staffs.
EX2: So late was it that there was no one in the office.
EX3: Such a hot day was it that no one wanted to work.
In conditional sentences

EX1: Were our staff better trained, we might make a larger profit.
EX2: Had I known about the vacancy, I would have applied.
With Be & Adjective

S + BE + SO + ADJ + THAT + S + V
→ SO + ADJ + BE + S + THAT + S + V + O
EX: The sea was so tough that the children could not go swimming.
→ So tough was the sea that the children could not go swimming.
With Verbs & Adverbs

S + VERB + SO + ADV + THAT + S + V
→ SO + ADV + Auxiliary Verb + S + V + THAT + S + V + O
EX: The children ran so quickly that no one can catch them.
→ So quickly did the children run that no one can catch them.
Pattern 1

S + BE + SUCH + (A/AN) + ADJ + N + THAT + CLAUSE
→ SUCH + BE (+ A/AN) + N + THAT + CLAUSE
EX: It is such a good book that I have read twice.
→ Such is a good book that I have read twice.
Pattern 2

S + BE + SUCH + (A/AN) + ADJ + N + THAT + CLAUSE
→ SUCH (+ A/AN) + N + BE + S + THAT + CLAUSE
EX: It is such a good book that I have read twice.
→ Such a good book is it that I have read twice.
Singular nouns & proper names, plural nouns not ending in s

Add 's
EX1: That's the manager's car, over there.
EX2: We're waiting for Sarah's decision about who to take on.
EX3: I think that women's rights should be protected by law.
Plural nouns ending in s, plural proper names

Add s' (just add apostrophe)
EX1: The workers' pay was increased by 10%
EX2: The Smiths' business eventually closed down.
Singular nouns ending in s

Add 's or s'
EX: The report is on Charles'(s) computer in the office.
Time expressions

Add 's or s' depending on singular/plural
EX1: After an hour's wait, I finally got to see manager.
EX2: I'll be at the factory in about ten minutes' time.
Nouns referring to people/groups, animals

Add 's
EX1: We held a meeting to discuss the department's finances.
EX2: The vet had a close look at the cat's paw.
Nouns not referring to people or animals (things)

Use 'of'
EX: The technician had a close look at the back of my laptop.
* Less preferred/incorrect: The technician had a close look at my laptop's back.
Compound nouns / joint possessives / phrases

Possessive 's goes at the end of the phrase
EX1: Tom, Dick and Harry's office is around here somewhere.
EX2: The president of France's visit to England will be good for business.
Possessive Adjectives
my, your, his, her, its, our, their

Indicates possession, always used before a noun.
EX: I'm really excited about my new job.
Possessive Pronouns
mine, yours, his, hers, ours, yours, theirs

Replaces a noun, or follows 'of' to indicate possession.
EX1: My new job is great. How about yours?
EX2: Are they customers of yours?
Note: Do not use an apostrophe in possessive pronouns (yours, hers, theirs, its).

vocabulary.meaning: to find or discover by searching or following information or evidence
vocabulary.example: We can trace the history of mankind back thousands of years.

vocabulary.meaning: the whole of the human race
vocabulary.example: The history of mankind is full of conflicts and achievements.

vocabulary.meaning: an unfair and unreasonable opinion or feeling, especially when formed without enough thought or knowledge
vocabulary.example: Laws exist to protect people from racial prejudice.

vocabulary.meaning: used when you want to make sure that someone does not misunderstand what you are saying
vocabulary.example: Don't get me wrong, I like the job, but the pay is too low.
